content:
# Numbers Chapter 10 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 159-160 | Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pa_0119-0120 (LXX)
**Source Images:** `NUM_010_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_010_page_002.jpg`; `NUM_010_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_010_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

### The Silver Trumpets (vv. 1-10)

**1** And YHWH spoke to Moses, saying,

**2** "Make for yourself two trumpets of silver; of hammered work you shall make them. And they shall be for you for the summoning of the congregation and for the setting out of the camps.

**3** And when they blow on both of them, all the congregation shall gather themselves to you at the entrance of the tent of meeting.

**4** And if they blow on one, then the leaders, the heads of the thousands of Israel, shall gather themselves to you.

**5** And when you blow an alarm, the camps that are encamped on the east side shall set out.

**6** And when you blow an alarm a second time, the camps that are encamped on the south side shall set out. An alarm shall be blown for their setting out.

**7** But when assembling the assembly, you shall blow, but you shall not sound an alarm.

**8** And the sons of Aaron, the priests, shall blow on the trumpets; and they shall be for you as a perpetual statute throughout your generations.

**9** And when you go to war in your land against the adversary who oppresses you, then you shall sound an alarm with the trumpets, and you shall be remembered before YHWH your God, and you shall be saved from your enemies.

**10** And on the day of your gladness, and at your appointed feasts, and at the beginnings of your months, you shall blow on the trumpets over your burnt offerings and over the sacrifices of your peace offerings; and they shall be for you a memorial before your God. I am YHWH your God."

---

### The Departure from Sinai (vv. 11-13)

**11** And it came to pass in the second year, in the second month, on the twentieth day of the month, the cloud was taken up from over the tabernacle of the testimony.

**12** And the Sons of Israel set out on their journeys from the wilderness of Sinai, and the cloud settled in the wilderness of Paran.

**13** And they set out at first at the mouth of YHWH by the hand of Moses.

---

### The Order of March (vv. 14-28)

**14** And the standard of the camp of the sons of Judah set out first, according to their armies, and over its army was Nahshon the son of Amminadab.

**15** And over the army of the tribe of the sons of Issachar was Nethanel the son of Zuar.

**16** And over the army of the tribe of the sons of Zebulun was Eliab the son of Helon.

**17** And the tabernacle was taken down, and the sons of Gershon and the sons of Merari set out, bearing the tabernacle.

**18** And the standard of the camp of Reuben set out, according to their armies, and over its army was Elizur the son of Shedeur.

**19** And over the army of the tribe of the sons of Simeon was Shelumiel the son of Zurishaddai.

**20** And over the army of the tribe of the sons of Gad was Eliasaph the son of Deuel.

**21** And the Kohathites set out, bearing the holy things; and the tabernacle was set up before their coming.

**22** And the standard of the camp of the sons of Ephraim set out, according to their armies, and over its army was Elishama the son of Ammihud.

**23** And over the army of the tribe of the sons of Manasseh was Gamaliel the son of Pedahzur.

**24** And over the army of the tribe of the sons of Benjamin was Abidan the son of Gideoni.

**25** And the standard of the camp of the sons of Dan set out, the rearguard for all the camps, according to their armies, and over its army was Ahiezer the son of Ammishaddai.

**26** And over the army of the tribe of the sons of Asher was Pagiel the son of Ochran.

**27** And over the army of the tribe of the sons of Naphtali was Ahira the son of Enan.

**28** These were the order of march of the Sons of Israel according to their armies. And they set out.

---

### Moses and Hobab (vv. 29-32)

**29** And Moses said to Hobab the son of Reuel the Midianite, the father-in-law of Moses, "We are setting out for the place of which YHWH said, 'I will give it to you.' Come with us and we will do good to you, for YHWH has spoken good concerning Israel."

**30** And he said to him, "I will not go, but rather to my own land and to my kindred I will go."

**31** And he said, "Please do not leave us, for you know our encamping in the wilderness, and you shall be for us as eyes.

**32** And it shall be, if you go with us, then whatever good YHWH does for us, we will do the same for you."

---

### The Ark Sets Out (vv. 33-36)

**33** And they set out from the mountain of YHWH a journey of three days, and the ark of the covenant of YHWH went before them a journey of three days, to seek out a resting place for them.

**34** And the cloud of YHWH was over them by day, when they set out from the camp.

**35** And it came to pass, when the ark set out, that Moses said, "Arise, O YHWH, and let your enemies be scattered, and let those who hate you flee before you."

**36** And when it rested, he said, "Return, O YHWH, to the ten thousands of the thousands of Israel."

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Textual Notes

1. **Verse 2 -- "trumpets of silver" (חֲצֹצְרֹת כֶּסֶף):** The chatsotsrot are distinct from the shofar (ram's horn). These are metallic instruments made of hammered (miqshah) silver, used exclusively by priests for liturgical and military signaling. The LXX renders σαλπιγγας αργυρας (silver trumpets). Archaeological parallels include the silver trumpets depicted on the Arch of Titus in Rome. The Hebrew root may be onomatopoeic, imitating the staccato sound of the instrument.

2. **Verse 2 -- "of hammered work" (מִקְשָׁה):** The same term used for the construction of the golden lampstand (Exodus 25:18, 31, 36). The trumpets were to be fashioned from a single piece of hammered silver, not cast or assembled from parts. The LXX renders ελατας (beaten/hammered). This specification elevates the trumpets to the level of sacred tabernacle furnishings.

3. **Verse 5 -- "blow an alarm" (תְּרוּעָה / תְּקַעְתֶּם תְּרוּעָה):** The Hebrew distinguishes between two types of trumpet sounds: the teqi'ah (a sustained blast, v. 7) used for assembly, and the teru'ah (a staccato or broken blast, vv. 5-6) used as an alarm for marching. The LXX renders σημασια (a signal), obscuring the acoustic distinction. The formal rendering preserves the functional difference between "blow" (for assembly) and "blow an alarm" (for departure).

4. **Verse 6 -- Missing direction in MT:** The Masoretic Text mentions only east (v. 5) and south (v. 6) for the alarm signals, leaving the west and north camps unmentioned. The LXX adds a third alarm for the west and a fourth for the north, apparently harmonizing the text with the four-camp arrangement of Numbers 2. The formal rendering follows the MT, which may assume the pattern is sufficiently clear from two examples, or which may reflect a textual lacuna.

5. **Verse 9 -- "you shall be remembered" (וְנִזְכַּרְתֶּם):** The Niphal of זכר. The theological subject of this remembering is God: the trumpet blasts serve as a means of bringing Israel before the divine attention. The LXX renders αναμνησθησεσθε (you shall be remembered). This "memorial" function of the trumpets parallels the memorial function described in verse 10, linking warfare and worship through the same instrument.

6. **Verse 10 -- "I am YHWH your God" (אֲנִי יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵיכֶם):** This divine self-identification formula, echoing the opening of the Decalogue (Exodus 20:2), closes the trumpet legislation. Its placement elevates the trumpet ordinance to covenantal status. The LXX renders εγω κυριος ο θεος υμων (I am the Lord your God), substituting the Tetragrammaton with κυριος as is standard in the Septuagint.

7. **Verse 11 -- "the second year, in the second month, on the twentieth day" (בַּשָּׁנָה הַשֵּׁנִית בַּחֹדֶשׁ הַשֵּׁנִי בְּעֶשְׂרִים בַּחֹדֶשׁ):** This precise date -- the twentieth day of the second month of the second year after the exodus -- marks the first movement from Sinai. The cloud had descended upon the tabernacle on the first day of the first month of the second year (Exodus 40:17), making the sojourn at Sinai approximately eleven months and twenty days from the initial arrival (Exodus 19:1). The LXX reads εν τω ετει τω δευτερω εν τω μηνι τω δευτερω εικαδι του μηνος. Both sources agree precisely on the date.

8. **Verse 12 -- "the wilderness of Paran" (מִדְבַּר פָּארָן):** Paran designates the large wilderness region in the northeastern Sinai peninsula, between the wilderness of Sinai to the south and the Negev to the north. The LXX reads εν τη ερημω Φαραν. This verse compresses what was likely a multi-stage journey (cf. Numbers 33:16-17, where Kibroth-hattaavah intervenes) into a single summary statement.

9. **Verse 13 -- "at first" (בָּרִאשֹׁנָה):** This may mean "for the first time" (i.e., this was the inaugural march following the trumpet legislation) or simply "first" in the order of narrative. The LXX reads πρωτοι (first). The formal rendering preserves the temporal ambiguity.

10. **Verse 17 -- "the tabernacle was taken down" (וְהוּרַד הַמִּשְׁכָּן):** The Hophal of ירד, literally "the tabernacle was caused to go down." The Gershonites and Merarites carried the structural components (curtains, frames, pillars) and departed ahead of the Kohathites (v. 21), who carried the holy vessels. This arrangement ensured the tabernacle structure would be erected and ready to receive the sacred objects when the Kohathites arrived at the new campsite. The LXX reads καθαιρειται η σκηνη (the tent is taken down).

11. **Verse 20 -- "Deuel" (דְּעוּאֵל):** The Leningrad Codex reads Deuel here, consistent with Numbers 1:14; 7:42, 47. In Numbers 2:14 the MT reads "Reuel" (רְעוּאֵל), likely a scribal confusion of dalet and resh, which are graphically similar in Hebrew script. The LXX reads Ραγουηλ (Raguel/Reuel) in all occurrences, suggesting the Vorlage may have read Reuel throughout. The formal rendering follows the MT of each verse.

12. **Verse 21 -- "the holy things" (הַמִּקְדָּשׁ):** The article with miqdash refers to the sacred objects: the ark, the table of showbread, the lampstand, and the altars. These were the Kohathites' responsibility (Numbers 4:4-15). The LXX reads τα ηγιασμενα (the sanctified things). The arrangement of the march -- Gershonites/Merarites first (v. 17), then Kohathites (v. 21) -- ensured structural readiness at the destination.

13. **Verse 25 -- "the rearguard" (מְאַסֵּף):** From the root אסף (to gather). The tribe of Dan served as the rear guard, gathering up stragglers and protecting the rear of the marching column. The LXX renders η σκηνη δαν πυλη παντων των παρεμβολων (the camp of Dan, the gate of all the camps), apparently reading or interpreting me'assef differently. The formal rendering follows the MT's military terminology.

14. **Verse 29 -- "Hobab the son of Reuel the Midianite, the father-in-law of Moses" (חֹבָב בֶּן רְעוּאֵל הַמִּדְיָנִי חֹתֵן מֹשֶׁה):** The identification of Moses' father-in-law is one of the most debated genealogical questions in the Pentateuch. Exodus 2:18 names him Reuel; Exodus 3:1 and 18:1 name him Jethro; here Hobab son of Reuel appears. The word choten can mean "father-in-law" or more broadly "in-law." The most natural reading of the Hebrew syntax is that Reuel is the father-in-law and Hobab is his son (thus Moses' brother-in-law), but Judges 4:11 calls Hobab the father-in-law of Moses. The LXX reads Ωβαβ υιω Ραγουηλ τω Μαδιανιτη γαμβρω Μωυση, where γαμβρος (in-law) is similarly ambiguous. The formal rendering preserves the MT's syntax without resolution.

15. **Verse 29 -- "YHWH has spoken good concerning Israel" (דִּבֶּר יְהוָה טוֹב עַל־יִשְׂרָאֵל):** Moses' appeal to Hobab is grounded in the reliability of the divine promise. The "good" (tov) YHWH has spoken refers to the land promise. The LXX reads κυριος ελαλησεν καλα περι Ισραηλ. This is one of the rare instances in Numbers where Moses engages in persuasion rather than command.

16. **Verse 31 -- "you shall be for us as eyes" (וְהָיִיתָ לָּנוּ לְעֵינָיִם):** A vivid metaphor: Hobab's desert knowledge would serve as Israel's eyes, guiding them through unfamiliar terrain. This is remarkable given that the cloud of YHWH directed the march (9:15-23). The coexistence of divine guidance and human expertise reflects a consistent biblical pattern in which providence does not exclude practical means. The LXX renders εση εν ημιν πρεσβυτης (you shall be among us as an elder), apparently reading or interpreting 'einayim differently, perhaps associating "eyes" with the oversight function of elders.

17. **Verse 33 -- "the mountain of YHWH" (מֵהַר יְהוָה):** Sinai is here called "the mountain of YHWH," a title used elsewhere for Zion (Isaiah 2:3; Micah 4:2; Psalm 24:3). This is the only place in the Pentateuch where Sinai receives this exact designation, elevating it to the highest category of sacred geography. The LXX reads εξ ορους κυριου (from the mountain of the Lord).

18. **Verse 33 -- "a journey of three days" (דֶּרֶךְ שְׁלֹשֶׁת יָמִים):** This phrase occurs twice in the verse, once for Israel's journey and once for the ark's advance reconnaissance. Whether the ark literally traveled ahead of the camp (contradicting its usual position among the Kohathites in the center) or whether this is a literary-theological statement about YHWH's vanguard protection has been debated. The LXX reads οδον τριων ημερων. The repetition emphasizes the ark's scouting function.

19. **Verses 35-36 -- The inverted nuns:** In the Masoretic tradition, these two verses are bracketed by inverted nun letters (׆), a unique scribal marking. The Talmud (Shabbat 115b-116a) records the opinion that these marks indicate that this passage is "not in its place" and belongs elsewhere, or that it constitutes an independent book -- which would mean the Torah contains seven books rather than five (Genesis, Exodus, Leviticus, Numbers-before-the-nuns, the two-verse book, Numbers-after-the-nuns, Deuteronomy). The Leningrad Codex preserves these marks. The LXX does not reflect any awareness of this scribal tradition.

20. **Verse 35 -- "Arise, O YHWH" (קוּמָה יְהוָה):** This battle cry is echoed in Psalm 68:2 [English 68:1] and Psalm 132:8. The imperative directed to YHWH is bold liturgical language, summoning the divine warrior to action. The LXX renders εξεγερθητι κυριε (arise, O Lord). The pairing of the ark's movement with this invocation identifies the ark as the throne or footstool of the divine warrior who marches at the head of his people.

21. **Verse 36 -- "Return, O YHWH, to the ten thousands of the thousands of Israel" (שׁוּבָה יְהוָה רִבְבוֹת אַלְפֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל):** The verb shuvah (return, rest) marks the cessation of march. The expression "ten thousands of the thousands" (rivevot alfei) is a superlative indicating a vast multitude. The LXX renders επιστρεφε κυριε χιλιαδας μυριαδας εν τω Ισραηλ (return, O Lord, to the thousands of myriads in Israel), reversing the order of the numbers. The formal rendering preserves the MT's word order. This verse envisions YHWH settling back among his innumerable people when the march concludes.

### Structural Observations

- Numbers 10 divides into two major sections that together frame Israel's departure from Sinai as both a liturgical and a military event. The trumpet legislation (vv. 1-10) provides the means of organization, while the march narrative (vv. 11-36) enacts what the legislation prescribed. The chapter thus forms a literary hinge between the Sinai legislation (Exodus 19 -- Numbers 10:10) and the wilderness journey narrative (Numbers 10:11 -- 21:35).

- The trumpet ordinance (vv. 1-10) establishes a comprehensive signal system covering four situations: assembly of the full congregation (v. 3), assembly of leaders only (v. 4), alarm for departure (vv. 5-6), and warfare and festival (vv. 9-10). The restriction of trumpet use to the Aaronic priests (v. 8) places all communal movement and military action under priestly -- and therefore divine -- authority.

- The march order (vv. 14-28) corresponds precisely to the camp arrangement of Numbers 2, now set in motion: Judah's division leads (east), followed by Reuben's division (south), then Ephraim's division (west), and finally Dan's division (north) as rearguard. The Levitical clans are interspersed: Gershonites and Merarites depart after Judah (v. 17), and Kohathites depart after Reuben (v. 21), ensuring the tabernacle structure arrives and is erected before the sacred objects arrive.

- The Hobab episode (vv. 29-32) introduces a striking tension: Israel has divine guidance through the cloud (9:15-23) and the ark (10:33), yet Moses earnestly seeks human assistance for navigation. The text does not resolve whether Hobab agreed -- the narrative simply moves on. This silence may be deliberate, leaving the reader to contemplate the relationship between divine sovereignty and human agency in Israel's journey.

- The two-verse poem (vv. 35-36) transforms the ark's physical movement into a theological drama. The setting out becomes a divine warrior's advance against enemies; the resting becomes YHWH's return to his people. These verses, marked by the inverted nuns, function as a miniature liturgy that sanctifies every stage of Israel's wilderness march -- departure and arrival, war and peace, scattering of foes and gathering of the faithful.
